title: Interview Opening Through the Storm
summary: Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill opens the recording at the Luper household in Oklahoma City on May 24, 2011, with filmmaker John Bishop, framing the session within the Library of Congress and Smithsonian NMAAHC Civil Rights History Project. Cal Luper welcomes them and jokes about the heavy weather outside, riffing on We Shall Overcome.
related_events: Civil Rights History Project interview series

title: Brother President and the New York Trip
summary: Cal recalls that his sister Marilyn Luper described the 1958 NAACP Youth Council trip to New York City with the play Brother President. He explains he still holds a film of the Brother President presentation, kept at a local Oklahoma City photo shop and being duplicated onto disc, and dates the performance to 1958.

title: A Family Affair in the Youth Council
summary: Cal describes the early Youth Council as a family affair where the whole household moved together because Clara said so. He names his cousin Betty Germany and his cousin Richard Brown, one of the original sit-inners, and notes the meetings happened in the Luper house.

title: The Sit-In Couch as Relic
summary: Cal describes the Luper living-room couch where Youth Council meetings were held, recovered around the 1980s after surviving the sit-in movement. He recalls it was scorched by a Molotov cocktail thrown through the front door and frames the recovered couch as a physical relic of the campaign.

title: Lunch Counters and the Hudson Elevator
summary: Memories return as Cal recalls a New York lunch counter where he could put his money in, choose his own sandwich, and be served, unlike home where he was told he could not eat. He remembers riding the elevator up and down in the Hudson hotel and walking its lobby without fear of being told to leave.

title: Downtown Segregation and Employment
summary: Cal describes how downtown Oklahoma City hotels the Skirvin and the Huckins were off limits to Black patrons unless they worked there, and how being a Luper made even that work hard to find. He reflects that getting employment was one of the difficult things he and his sibling faced during the campaign.

title: Katz Drug Store at Main and Robinson
summary: Cal recalls the first nights at Katz Drug Store, walking across Main Street and entering through the doorway on the Main Street side, with Main and Robinson running east to west and Broadway north to south. He notes the site is now the home of the Ford Theater and that passing it always returns him to the mornings and evenings spent there.

title: Grandmother's Commute From Carverdale
summary: After noting he was never allowed to try on hats or shoes downtown, Cal turns to his grandmother, who lived in the Carverdale addition, a mostly Black neighborhood near Northeast Tenth and what is now Martin Luther King. He describes her daily two-bus commute with a transfer near Johnny Browns, gives her address as 1236 Windermere, and remembers her as a willing babysitter.

title: Self-Discipline and the Commanders
summary: Cal explains the self-discipline the Youth Council maintained within the city and describes an organization called the Commanders, whose job was both to keep outsiders from harassing the demonstrators and to keep members from misbehaving on the inside.

title: Schoolbooks at the Lunch Counter
summary: Cal recalls his mother insisting that members bring schoolbooks to sit-ins at Katz Drug Store and Bishop's Restaurant, which locked its doors against demonstrators. Those who got inside used the time to study, and Cal worked on his first year of Spanish while keeping watch over his sister and the younger girls, including the one everyone nicknamed Baby.

title: Clara Luper's Public Bearing
summary: Asked what an observer would have seen in his mother, Cal describes how she met restaurant owners, governors, mayors, and police chiefs by looking them straight in the face rather than performing the deferential shuffle expected of Black Oklahomans at the time.

title: The Clara Luper Radio Show
summary: Cal describes working with his mother on the Clara Luper radio show at the station on Southeast 44th and Bryant for more than thirty years. He recalls a comedic call-and-response format, likened to Amos and Andy, where she threw out a topic and he batted it back, and notes the family still holds hundreds of tapes.

title: Oklahoma University and the Electrical Trade
summary: Cal describes studying history at the University of Oklahoma while distracted by thoughts of his mother and sister back home, which hurt his grades. He left for trade school for electrical work, earned a license, and worked alongside his father, also an electrician, for a couple of years before his father's death.

title: Weighing Nonviolence Against Black Power
summary: Cal recalls that after gaining weapons proficiency in military service he was tempted toward Black Power organizing, but argues against armed confrontation: a fist cannot beat a gun, while nonviolence reaches a person's heart. He observes that even nonviolent activists studied judo because there is always somebody tougher, and concludes he would rather concede a point than be beaten to prove one.

title: Family Cost and the Morning Routine
summary: Asked whether the family paid too high a price, Cal reflects that the question has crossed his mind over the last twenty years, while noting how the sit-in routine set a lifelong rhythm of rising and being ready by nine. He recalls meeting at Calvary Baptist under pastor Reverend Morris Kerry, close enough to walk to Katz Drug Store, Bishop's, or Johnny Browns, where many photographs of the children were taken.

title: Ministers Bratzinger and Alexander
summary: Asked about his mother's key allies, Cal says he thinks everyone mattered to her, then names Reverend Bratzinger, a minister at Fifth Street Baptist, and Reverend Alexander, the AME minister at the church at 13th and Kelham, both of whom Clara would stop whatever she was doing to talk with.
related_events: Oklahoma City civil rights ministerial network

title: Dr. Charles N. Atkins and Hannah Atkins
summary: Cal describes Dr. Charles N. Atkins, a Bahamian-born physician who debated strategy intensely with his mother, helped with her speeches and with keeping the nonviolent discipline right, brought many babies into the world, and treated arthritis on the East Side. He notes Atkins was married to Hannah Atkins, who became an Oklahoma state representative, and that their son Ed was a close friend.
related_events: Oklahoma City civil rights ministerial network

title: Melvin B. Porter Boards the Bus
summary: Cal recounts meeting Melvin B. Porter on the return from the Brother President performance, when the bus passed through Nashville and Porter, then at Vanderbilt Law School, saw the Oklahoma banner, dashed aboard, and pledged to open a law firm in Oklahoma City. Porter did so and rose to the State Senate, where he argued issues eloquently on the floor, and his sons Melvin Jr. and Joel continue the firm on Northwest 23rd and Hudson.

title: Sam Cornelius and the East Side YMCA
summary: Cal remembers Sam Cornelius, director of the East Side YMCA, where the Youth Council held Monday-night meetings before the Freedom Center opened. He recalls Cornelius's family, his son Bruce and his daughter Charlene, a friend and Douglass High School classmate of Cal's who lived out in Garden Oaks, and Mrs. Cornelius as a second mother who would report any misbehavior to Clara.

title: The Board of Education Joke
summary: Cal explains the family joke of the Board of Education, his mother's wooden disciplining board, made before the era of child-abuse concerns and broken in well during the sit-in movement. The board was carved by Alexander Jones, a mechanical arts teacher at Dunjee School who taught woodworking and carpentry, commuted with Clara from the 1500 block of Northeast Park to her 1800 block home, and drove her because her car was not running.

title: Professor McClure and Closing
summary: Cal recalls Professor McClure, a man older than his mother who was part of the group that helped desegregate the university, though Cal did not know him personally and remembers a relative who was often around. The interview ends with the standard Library of Congress and Smithsonian NMAAHC sign-off.
